1: The wilderness and dry land will be glad; the desert will rejoice and blossom like a crocus. Isaiah 35 opens with a creation-reversal oracle: the very terrain of judgment (desert/wilderness as the wasteland of exile and curse) becomes the terrain of rejoicing. The crocus blossoming in the desert is the anti-entropy of the Creator's intervention — what was dying now blooms. In the NT, the wilderness is where John the Baptist prepares the way (Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4, John 1:23, all citing Isaiah 40:3 — the twin chapter of Isaiah 35): the desert itself is the site of eschatological preparation. Jesus' forty-day wilderness testing (Matthew 4:1-11) inverts the wilderness-of-curse: where Israel failed in the desert, the Son succeeds, beginning the transformation of the wilderness into gladness.
2: It will blossom abundantly and rejoice with joy and singing. The glory of Lebanon will be given to it, the majesty of Carmel and Sharon — they will see the glory of YHWH, the majesty of our God. The glory given to the desert is the glory of Lebanon (cedars, lush forests), Carmel (fertile coastland), and Sharon (the coastal plain of flowers): the most verdant regions of the land are now the desert's identity. The purpose: they will see the glory of YHWH. This is the theophanic climax — the transformation of landscape is the medium by which YHWH's glory becomes visible. In the NT, the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we saw his glory (John 1:14): the glory of YHWH visible in the Incarnation is the same glory that transforms the wilderness in this verse. Jesus' transfiguration (Matthew 17:2) is the concentrated, visible manifestation of this glory.
3: Strengthen the weak hands and make firm the feeble knees. The command to the community to strengthen one another for the journey ahead. The weak hands and feeble knees are the physical postures of exhaustion and despair — those who have lost the ability to walk or work under the weight of exile's suffering. The writer to the Hebrews quotes this verse directly (Hebrews 12:12: therefore strengthen your weak hands and your feeble knees) as the ethical call to endurance under discipline: the Isaianic strengthening-for-the-journey becomes the NT call to perseverance under the cross. The context in Heb 12 is the race of faith — running with endurance the race set before us (Hebrews 12:1-2) — which is the NT form of the highway-walking in vv 8-10.
4: Say to those with a fearful heart: Be strong! Do not be afraid! Your God will come with vengeance — with divine retribution he will come to save you. The double comfort: the reassurance (be strong, do not fear) precedes the theological ground (your God will come). YHWH's coming has two faces — vengeance toward the enemy and salvation toward the faithful — and both are part of the same advent. In the NT, Jesus's first coming is the saving advent; his second coming completes the vengeance-and-salvation unity. John the Baptist's question from prison (Matthew 11:3: are you the coming one?) is answered with the signs of vv 5-6 — the saving-advent evidence. But the full vengeance-advent awaits the parousia: then the kings of the earth and the great and generals and the powerful will hide from the face of the one seated on the throne (Revelation 6:15-17).
5: Then the eyes of the blind will be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped. This verse is the pivot of the chapter: the physical healing of blindness and deafness is both the sign and the substance of the coming salvation. The blind eyes and stopped ears of Isaiah 29:9-12 (the judgment: YHWH poured out a spirit of deep sleep) are now reversed by YHWH's own coming. Jesus applies this verse explicitly to himself when answering John's question: go tell John what you hear and see — the blind receive sight and the deaf hear (Matthew 11:4-5, Luke 7:22), directly citing this verse. The healing miracles of Jesus are not incidental demonstrations of power but are the prophetic sign-fulfillments of YHWH's arriving salvation — the advent-evidence that Isaiah 35 predicted. The connection to Isaiah 29:18 (in that day the deaf will hear the words of a book, and the eyes of the blind will see) makes the healing pattern a central Isaianic motif.
6: Then the lame will leap like a deer and the mute tongue will shout for joy. For water will burst out in the wilderness and streams in the desert. The lame-leaping and mute-shouting continue Jesus's self-citation in Matthew 11:5 (the lame walk, the mute speak): all four healings of v5-6a are the Isaianic checklist of Messianic arrival. The Acts account of the lame man healed at the Beautiful Gate (Acts 3:8: he jumped to his feet and began to walk, leaping and praising God) is explicitly an echo of this verse — the lame man leaping is what Isaiah said would happen when YHWH came to save. The bursting-water-in-the-wilderness (v6b) is the creation reversal that provides the physical environment for the redeemed journey (vv 8-10).
7: The burning sand will become a pool and the thirsty ground bubbling springs. In the haunts where jackals once lay, grass and reeds and papyrus will grow. The wilderness-reversal continues: the mirage of burning sand (sharab — the shimmer of heat that deceives the thirsty) becomes actual water; the habitat of jackals (death-associated scavengers, the wilderness-dwellers) becomes the habitat of papyrus and reeds (Nile-delta water plants — the anti-Egypt: not the forced labor of Egypt's waters but the free abundance of YHWH's provision). Jesus identifies himself as the fulfillment of the thirsty-ground promise: if anyone is thirsty, let him come to me and drink — rivers of living water will flow from within him (John 7:37-38). The burning sand becoming a pool is what Jesus gives to the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well: living water that becomes a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14).
8: A highway will be there — it will be called the Way of Holiness (derek hakodesh). The unclean will not travel on it; it will be for those who walk on the Way — fools will not wander on it. The highway of holiness is the infrastructure of the eschatological return: YHWH prepares a way (the same word as Isaiah 40:3, from which John the Baptist is identified as the voice crying in the wilderness, prepare the way of YHWH). The Way of Holiness is the path available only to the redeemed — not geographically exclusive but covenantally exclusive: the unclean cannot travel on it. Jesus is the fulfillment not only as the one who builds the way but as the Way itself: I am the way and the truth and the life (John 14:6). The early church was called the Way (Acts 9:2, Isaiah 19:9, Isaiah 19:23, Isaiah 24:14, Isaiah 24:22) — the followers of the one who is the derek hakodesh.
9: No lion will be there; no fierce lion will go up on it; they will not be found there. But the redeemed will walk there. The highway secured from predators: the dangers of the wilderness (lions, wild beasts) that threaten the journey are removed by YHWH's protection. The redeemed walk in safety. In the NT, the protection of the redeemed on their journey is expressed as the Shepherd's keeping: I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand (John 10:28). The adversary who prowls around like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour (1 Peter 5:8) is the NT lion-on-the-highway — but the redeemed on the Way of Holiness are protected by the Lion of Judah himself (Revelation 5:5) who has overcome the devouring lion.
10: And the ransomed of YHWH will return and come to Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee. The ransomed (peduyei YHWH) are those purchased back from exile — the same language as Isaiah 51:11 (its near-verbatim repetition) and the NT's redemption vocabulary. They come to Zion with singing: the journey concludes at worship. The everlasting joy as a crown is the counterpart to the crown of thorns (Matthew 27:29) — the mock-crown of suffering reversed to the crown of gladness. The sorrow-and-sighing-fleeing is quoted nearly verbatim in Revelation 21:4: he will wipe every tear from their eyes; there will be no more death, mourning, crying, or pain, for the old order has passed away. Isaiah 35:10 is one of the most direct OT antecedents of the new Jerusalem's everlasting-joy promise — the ransomed of YHWH in the new Zion is the eschatological gathering of the redeemed in the city of the Lamb.
